dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for a fall at the residence.
Hi, I'm Brad, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened today?
I fell in the bathroom. My left leg is really hurting.
On a scale of alertness, would you say you're aware of where you are, the current date, and who I am?
Yes, it's Friday, I'm in Richmond, and you're an EMS provider.
That means you are oriented to person, place, time, and events-clearly A&O x 4.
Can you move all your extremities to the best of your ability?
I can't move my left leg, but the right side moves fine.
Let me check your airway. You sound fine. Can you speak comfortably?
Yes, my throat is fine.
Your breathing is steady and regular. No signs of difficulty.
Let me listen to your lungs. They sound clear and equal bilaterally.
Skin is warm and dry. Pulses are present, but cap refill is delayed over 4 seconds.
Blood pressure is 79 over 48, pulse 89, and respirations 16.
Oxygen saturation is at 99% via pulse oximetry.
Blood glucose is 116. No hypoglycemia symptoms are being observed per ePCR.
EKG performed. Sinus rhythm present.
Can you describe what happened during the fall?
I was walking to the bathroom when I felt orthostatic hypotension hit. I became dizzy and fell.
Did you manage to reach the emergency call button after falling?
Yes, I had to slide across the floor to reach it before security could respond.
Did you hit your head, chest, or back during the fall?
No injuries to my head, neck, or back. Everything else is fine.
What sensations or abilities are affected right now? Can you feel or move much of your left side?
I can't feel much in my thigh. My foot still has some sensation, but it's not good.
I'll check for any signs of bruising, swelling, or skin abnormalities on your lower left limb.
There is no bruising or rash observed, but capillary refill is greater than 4 seconds.
How's your neck and head? Any pain or difficulty moving them?
No problems. Neck is flexible and not painful.
Any drainage from your ears, nose, or mouth? Any facial swelling or pain?
No drainage, no swelling, and the facial features are symmetric.
I'll listen to your chest now. No chest pain reported.
Breath sounds are clear bilaterally. Heart sounds are regular and strong.
Let me assess your abdomen. Any pain or distention?
No. It's soft and pain-free.
I see a closed fracture to the left thigh, hip. Any open wounds?
No wounds. The fracture is closed.
Your GCS score is 15. You are conscious and responsive.
Your glucose remains at zero. Per ePCR, we are moving forward with IV hydration to maintain circulation.
EKG monitoring shows normal sinus rhythm.
Oxygen saturation remains at 99%.
An IV line has been started in your right forearm to begin normal saline hydration.
We are giving you a normal saline bolus to help with hypotension.
A 200cc bolus of normal saline is being administered. It should help your blood pressure.
Blood pressure improved to 92 over 53 after the bolus.
Next reading shows blood pressure of 61 over 35-this is a drop but expected due to presumed femoral artery compromise.
Oxygen saturation is now at 94%.
Your respiration rate is 16, no changes noted.
We will continue to monitor it closely.
Because your blood pressure is improving slightly, we can now give 50 mcg of Fentanyl to help with the pain.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm.
Blood pressure improved to 104 over 64 after the bolus.
Oxygen saturation is now at 98%.
Blood pressure has dropped again to 67 over 44. Increasing oxygen to 2LPM.
I will start a second IV in your left wrist. More fluids will be administered.
We are using a sheet to ease you into a better transfer position with minimal movement.
Your left side is being closely monitored. Pulses are still present.
Still reading zero on the glucose meter.
O2 is back up to 99%. Respiratory rate is 16 and regular.
EKG is sinus rhythm continuously, no changes observed.
On a 1-10 scale, how would you rate the pain now?
It's an 8 now. Decreased since the Fentanyl.
Skin temperature and color of the right leg are normal.
Left thigh and hip are deformed-stabilized in the current position. Slight sensation remains in the foot.
Your respiratory rate has increased to 18. We will continue to track it.
Blood pressure has increased to 156 over 124-higher than usual, possibly from stress or pain.
I will apply safety straps to your lower body now to ensure it remains secure during transport.
We will transport you to VCU Medical Center for emergency orthopedic and trauma care.
Oxygen saturation has returned to 100%. You are breathing normally.
Still around an 8? Has the pain changed or increased?
Still around an 8. It's somewhat manageable now.
Good signs that circulation is still present. No open fractures-just a closed break.
Past medical issues include chronic pain, anxiety, hyperlipidemia, GERD/Reflux, and both hypertension and hypotension.
We are following General - Pain Control (Protocol 3-10).
EKG monitor continues showing normal sinus rhythm.
You are now being carefully loaded onto the stretcher and placed on your right side.
You are secured with safety restraints. Your left side is carefully padded.
Let me check one more time for vitals.
Pulse is 85, respirations 18, blood pressure is 106 over 66.
Oxygen at 98%, and Fentanyl was administered as planned.
We are calling the ED ahead-we expect arrival in the trauma bay within minutes.
ED transfer phone call has been made. Receiving team is ready.
We are now entering the ED building for trauma bay 3 as instructed.
Receiving team is present. Report will now be provided.
Two IV lines in place. Fentanyl 50 mcg dose was last administered. Glucose remains undetectable.
Receiving nurse acknowledges our report and transfer is complete.
Patient turned over successfully. EMS care complete at 00:13.
Ambulance is being restocked for next call. All procedures complete.
Let me out of this one. Keep us updated on the ED progress.
All protocols followed. Report filed.
We'll wait while you document one last time before leaving the scene.
Final report submitted. All signed and checked. Cleaned up everything.
We're all set. Ready to head back.
All done for this call. Next one awaits.
We used improvised positioning with a sheet to slide you from the bathroom to the hallway. This minimized movement and injury risk.
You're currently in a position we improvised with available materials to avoid worsening the leg injury.
